title: Universal tracer statistics in single-file transport
authors: Soumyabrata Saha, Jitendra Kethepalli, Benjamin Guiselin, Jacopo De Nardis, Tridib Sadhu
abstract: We uncover an emergent universality in the large-scale, long-time statistics of a one-dimensional hard-rod gas evolving under two fundamentally different classes of microscopic dynamics: stochastic (diffusive) and unitary (ballistic). Remarkably, despite the difference of the two systems, the one-time joint distribution of the positions of multiple tracers exhibits identical non-Gaussian fluctuations, up to a simple dynamical scaling. This universality holds in both annealed and quenched ensembles, demonstrating a persistent memory of the initial state. Differences between the dynamics manifest at large scales only in multi-time statistics. Our conclusions are based on explicit large-deviation results for the one-time statistics of tracer pairs and the two-time statistics of a single tracer. Similar physics extends to current fluctuations, demonstrated explicitly in the quenched ensemble. We obtain these results from exact microscopic solutions for both dynamics and, independently, from fluctuating hydrodynamics in the ballistic case in the annealed ensemble. Our rare-event simulations further corroborate these findings and provide a novel demonstration of sampling atypical fluctuations in both types of hard-rod gas.
summary: This paper demonstrates a surprising universality in single-file transport where fundamentally different microscopic dynamics—stochastic diffusion and ballistic motion—produce identical one-time statistics for tracer particles. By using both exact analytical methods and advanced rare-event simulations, the authors show that the large-deviation functions for particle positions and currents are the same up to scaling. This universality holds for both annealed and quenched initial conditions, though the distinction between the two dynamics is preserved in multi-time correlations and aging effects.
main_problem: Investigating whether an emergent universality exists in the large-scale, long-time statistics of tracer particles in single-file transport, specifically comparing stochastic (diffusive) and unitary (ballistic) dynamics.
main_result: The authors uncover that the one-time joint distribution of multiple tracers exhibits identical non-Gaussian fluctuations for both diffusive and ballistic gases, up to a simple dynamical scaling, though differences appear in multi-time statistics.
method: The study employs exact microscopic solutions, hydrodynamic approaches (MFT and BMFT), and rare-event numerical simulations using importance sampling and multi-histogram reweighting.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional hard-rod gas with finite particle length 'a' subject to either diffusive (Brownian) or ballistic (velocity-exchange) dynamics in both annealed and quenched ensembles.
key_observables: One-time tracer position, joint distribution of tracer pairs, two-time statistics of a single tracer, and time-integrated current.
important_parameters: Scaling exponent H (1/4 for diffusive, 1/2 for ballistic), rod length 'a', density 'rho', and diffusion coefficient 'D0'.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the large-time and large-system limits and relies on a coordinate transformation mapping hard rods to non-interacting point particles.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows schematic trajectories of diffusive vs. ballistic motion; Figure 2 presents numerical and analytical results for the Large Deviation Function (LDF) of tracers and currents, as well as equal-time and two-time covariances.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of universality in single-file transport, details the two dynamical models, presents analytical derivations using microscopic and hydrodynamic methods, provides numerical validation via rare-event simulations, and concludes with discussion on the breakdown of universality in multi-time statistics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic trajectories of the (a) diffusive and (b) ballistic hard-rod gas. All rods have equal length a and unit mass. In (a), particles undergo Brownian motion between collisions, while in (b) they move along straight lines governed by their velocities, which are exchanged upon collision. The trajectories of two tracers are highlighted, with their positions at time t denoted by zt and yt.

Figure 2. Results for both gases in the quenched ensemble with D0 = 0.5, a = 1, and ¯ρ = 0.25. Markers (D: diffusive, B: ballistic) are numerical results; solid lines are analytical predictions. Plot (a) shows the tracer LDF (2c) and the current LDF,
